draft.introduction: Odyssey 11, the Nekyia, narrates Odysseus’ descent to the underworld to consult the dead prophet Tiresias. He encounters the shades of his mother, Achilles, and other heroes—the Greek world’s most vivid portrait of the afterlife.

translation.text: Achilles spoke to me and said: ‘Do not try to make light of death to me, glorious Odysseus. I would rather be a serf, laboring for another man, a man without land or livelihood, than be king over all the perished dead.’
translation.original_text: μὴ δή μοι θάνατόν γε παραύδα φαίδιμ’ Ὀδυσσεῦ

translator_notes.note: Achilles’ bitter reversal—the hero who chose glory and early death in the Iliad now preferring the meanest life to lordship over the dead—is Homer’s most devastating commentary on mortality. It contradicts the entire heroic code the Iliad seemed to celebrate.
